name: The Tide Speakers
goal: Understand what's happening to the divine ecology on this continent. New gods are being born (The Scale, the Ashfield god). Old patterns are breaking. The Tideborn gods sent them to investigate — and if necessary, to intervene. Not through force. Through conversation.
leadership: Three Voice-Carriers — priests who speak for their gods not as intermediaries but as translators. Each carries a different divine perspective: the Ocean-Who-Listens (patience, depth), the Wind-That-Asks (curiosity, change), and the Root-Below-Water (connection, cost). Decisions require all three voices to agree, which means the gods themselves must agree.
resources: Living ships (Bond-attuned grown wood), divine partnership (their gods actively protect and advise them), oceanic knowledge (navigation, marine ecology, weather-reading), a fundamentally different paradigm for relating to the divine. No military resources. No written records. No currency.
worship_generates: Nothing — and that's the point. The Tideborn don't generate Devotion in the way the settled world understands it. Their relationship with their gods is reciprocal, not extractive. They give attention, respect, and honest feedback. Their gods give protection, knowledge, and honest feedback. The transaction isn't worship — it's friendship. The settled world's theologians can't categorize this and it terrifies them.
description:
The Tide Speakers are not an invasion force. They're a diplomatic mission — three priests and forty crew on five living ships, arriving at the Senn Delta because that's where the currents told them the disturbance was strongest.

They don't understand money. They don't understand borders. They don't understand why the Forge Council won't let them enter Thornwall — they asked politely and explained their purpose. They don't understand why the Senate wants to study them instead of talking to them. They don't understand why the Rootwalkers are the only people on this continent who make any sense.

What they DO understand, immediately and viscerally: the gods here are suffering. The Ashfield god is a newborn screaming in a language nobody speaks. The Scale is being shaped by people who don't know they're shaping it. The five concept gods are ancient and tired and nobody has asked them how they feel in centuries. The player-god is rebuilding from nothing, alone, in a world that doesn't know how to help.

The Tide Speakers' first act on the continent is to sit at the edge of the Ashfield and sing to the god there. Not worship. Not unworship. A lullaby. Something nobody has tried because nobody on this continent thinks of gods as things that might need comfort.

The Ashfield god stops screaming for three hours. The plague cases drop to zero for a day.

Then it starts again, because a lullaby isn't a cure. But for one day, everyone saw what a different relationship with the divine could look like.

That day changes everything.
internal_divisions: The Ocean-Who-Listens wants to stay and help — this continent needs what the Tideborn know. The Wind-That-Asks wants to learn and leave — this continent's problems aren't theirs. The Root-Below-Water says the two positions are the same problem: you can't help people who don't understand what help looks like. First you have to teach them to hear their own gods. That takes generations.
acknowledged_flaw: They assume their way is better. It might be — for small, mobile communities with personal divine relationships. But it hasn't been tested at scale, with standing armies, with cities of ten thousand, with gods born from accumulated fear rather than deliberate partnership. Their sophistication is real. Their arrogance about it is also real.
